practices: The AP Chemistry exam assesses six Science Practices. Practice 1: Models and Representations (MCQ 8-12%, FRQ 2-4%) involves describing and using models. Practice 2: Question and Method (MCQ 8-12%, FRQ 10-16%) focuses on determining scientific questions and methods. Practice 3: Representing Data and Phenomena (FRQ 8-16%) involves creating representations of chemical phenomena and is not assessed in the multiple-choice section. Practice 4: Model Analysis (MCQ 23-30%, FRQ 5-9%) requires analyzing and interpreting models. Practice 5: Mathematical Routines (MCQ 35-42%, FRQ 43-53%) is about solving problems using mathematical relationships. Practice 6: Argumentation (MCQ 8-12%, FRQ 15-24%) involves developing explanations and scientific arguments.

keyTopics: Calculate quantities using the mole concept and dimensional analysis
Explain the relationship between mass spectrum data and elemental isotopes
Represent the ground-state electron configuration of an atom or ion
Explain the relationship between photoelectron spectroscopy (PES) and electron configuration
Explain trends in atomic properties (e.g., ionization energy, atomic radii) using Coulomb's law and the shell model
Explain the relationship between trends in reactivity and periodicity
Explain the relationship between bond type (ionic, covalent, metallic) and electronegativity
Represent the relationship between potential energy and internuclear distance
Represent an ionic solid with a particulate model consistent with Coulomb's law
Represent a molecule with a Lewis diagram, including accounting for resonance and formal charge
Explain structural and electron properties of molecules using VSEPR theory and hybridization (sp, sp2, sp3)
Represent a metallic solid and/or alloy using a particulate model
Explain the relationship between chemical structures and the relative strength of intermolecular forces (London dispersion, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding, ion-dipole)
Explain the relationship among macroscopic properties, particulate-level structure, and interactions in solids, liquids, and gases
Explain gas behavior using the ideal gas law (PV=nRT) and kinetic molecular theory (KMT)
Explain non-ideal gas behavior based on interparticle forces and volumes
Explain the results of a separation experiment (e.g., chromatography, distillation) based on intermolecular interactions
Explain the amount of light absorbed by a solution using the Beer-Lambert law (A=εbc)
Represent changes in matter with balanced molecular, complete ionic, and net ionic equations
Represent a chemical reaction with a consistent particulate model
Explain changes in amounts of reactants and products based on the balanced reaction equation (stoichiometry)
Identify the equivalence point in a titration based on the amounts of titrant and analyte
Identify a reaction as acid-base, oxidation-reduction, or precipitation
Represent a balanced redox reaction equation using half-reactions
Explain the relationship between the rate of a chemical reaction and experimental parameters
Represent experimental data with a consistent rate law expression
Identify the rate law expression from data showing concentration changes over time (integrated rate laws for zeroth, first, and second order)
Explain reaction rates in terms of the collision model (frequency, energy, orientation of collisions)
Represent the activation energy and overall energy change in an elementary reaction using a reaction energy profile
Identify the components of a reaction mechanism (reactants, intermediates, products, catalysts)
Explain the relationship between a catalyst's effect and changes in the reaction mechanism
Explain energy changes in a system (endothermic and exothermic processes)
Explain heat transfer and thermal equilibrium based on molecular collisions
Calculate the heat (q) absorbed or released by a system using calorimetry (q=mcΔT)
Explain changes in heat (q) during a phase transition
Calculate the enthalpy change of a reaction based on average bond energies
Calculate the enthalpy change for a process based on standard enthalpies of formation
Explain the relationship between the enthalpy of a process and the sum of enthalpies of individual steps (Hess's Law)
Represent the reaction quotient (Qc or Qp) and the corresponding equilibrium expressions (Kc or Kp)
Explain the relationship between very large or very small values of K and the relative concentrations of species at equilibrium
Represent a multistep process with an overall equilibrium expression using constituent K expressions
Identify the concentrations or partial pressures of species at equilibrium based on initial conditions and K
Identify the response of a system at equilibrium to an external stress using Le Châtelier's principle
Calculate the solubility of a salt based on its Ksp value
Identify the solubility of a salt based on the concentration of a common ion
Calculate the values of pH and pOH based on Kw and concentrations
Explain the relationship among pH, pOH, and concentrations of species in a solution of a weak acid or weak base (Ka and Kb)
Explain the relationship among concentrations of major species in a mixture of weak and strong acids and bases
Explain results from the titration of a mono- or polyprotic acid or base solution
Explain the relationship between the strength of an acid or base and the structure of the molecule or ion
Explain the relationship between the predominant form of a weak acid/base in solution at a given pH and the pKa
Explain the relationship between the ability of a buffer to stabilize pH and the reactions that occur when an acid or base is added
Identify the pH of a buffer solution based on the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation
Identify the sign and relative magnitude of the entropy change (ΔS) associated with chemical or physical processes
Explain whether a process is thermodynamically favored based on an evaluation of Gibbs free energy (ΔG° = ΔH° - TΔS°)
Explain the relationship between a process being thermodynamically favored and its relationship with K, ΔG°, and T (ΔG° = -RTlnK)
Explain why a thermodynamically favored reaction might not occur at a measurable rate (kinetic control)
Explain the relationship between the physical components and operational principles of an electrochemical cell (galvanic and electrolytic)
Explain whether an electrochemical cell is thermodynamically favored based on its standard cell potential (ΔG° = -nFE°)
Qualitatively explain the relationship between deviations from standard cell conditions and changes in cell potential (Nernst equation concept)
Calculate the amount of charge flow based on changes in reactants and products in an electrochemical cell (Faraday's laws)

examFormat.freeResponse: 7 questions (3 long-response and 4 short-response), 105 minutes, 50% of total score

antiPatterns: The CED explicitly de-emphasizes or excludes several topics from assessment. Students are not required to perform calculations involving the Arrhenius equation, the Nernst equation (though qualitative understanding is required), or conversions between Kc and Kp. Rote memorization of extensive solubility rules beyond a basic set is not required. Other excluded topics include assigning quantum numbers, writing electron configurations for exceptions to the Aufbau principle, molecular orbital theory, hybridization involving d-orbitals, interpreting phase diagrams, colligative property calculations, Lewis acid-base concepts, and the technical distinctions between enthalpy and internal energy. The terms 'reducing agent' and 'oxidizing agent' are also not assessed.
